title: Doratoxylon chouxii
intro: Doratoxylon chouxii, also known as the New Caledonian ironwood, is a species of flowering plant in the family Euphorbiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, as well as potential uses in treating various conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Doratoxylon chouxii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, with the most common herbal preparations including a tea made from the bark and roots, as well as a topical paste or ointment used to treat skin conditions.
taxonomy: Doratoxylon chouxii is a species of plant in the family Sapindaceae. It is native to the Philippines and is a type of tree. The classification of Doratoxylon chouxii includes its kingdom as Plantae, phylum as Magnoliophyta, class as Magnoliopsida, order as Sapindales, and family as Sapindaceae. The genus of the plant is Doratoxylon. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and evolutionary relationships with other plants.
uses: Doratoxylon chouxii has been traditionally used in the Pacific Islands for its medicinal properties, particularly for treating various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its bark and roots are used in folk remedies to make tea and infusions, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The plant is also used to treat wounds and to reduce pain and swelling. Additionally, it is used to treat respiratory issues such as coughs and colds.
